content.text: A tokeniser's vocabulary is the complete set of token strings the model knows. Every token maps to a unique integer ID, and those IDs are the only inputs a language model ever sees. The vocabulary size is one of the most consequential design decisions in building a language model: it determines the embedding matrix dimensions, controls how many tokens are needed to represent any given text, and sets the trade-off between sequence efficiency and parameter cost. GPT-2 used 50,257 tokens; BERT uses 30,522; LLaMA 3 jumped to 128,256; GPT-4's cl100k_base sits at 100,277. Each choice reflects a deliberate balance.
Vocabulary Size Trade-offs
The vocabulary appears in two places in a transformer: the embedding matrix at the input (maps token ID → vector of dimension
d_model
) and the unembedding matrix at the output (maps the final hidden state → logits over all vocabulary items). Both have shape
[vocab_size, d_model]
and are typically weight-tied (the same matrix, transposed). For LLaMA 3 with
vocab_size=128256
and
d_model=4096
, this matrix alone is 128,256 × 4,096 × 2 bytes ≈ 1 GB of the model's weight.
Dimension
Larger vocabulary
Smaller vocabulary
Sequence length
Fewer tokens per text — fits more content in the context window
More tokens per text — context fills up faster
Embedding matrix size
Larger — more parameters at input/output layers
Smaller — parameter-efficient at input/output
Rare-word coverage
Rare words more likely to be single tokens
Rare words split into more subword pieces
Training data per token
Each token seen fewer times — rare tokens undertrained
Each token seen more times — denser training signal
Softmax cost
More expensive — compute over more logits at each step
Cheaper softmax — fewer logits
Vocabulary Sizes in Major Models

from transformers import AutoTokenizer
import tiktoken

# Vocabulary sizes of major tokenisers
vocab_sizes = {
    "bert-base-uncased":   30_522,   # WordPiece
    "t5-small":            32_100,   # SentencePiece Unigram
    # LLaMA 2:             32_000,   # SentencePiece BPE
    "gpt2":                50_257,   # Byte-level BPE (50K + 1 <|endoftext|>)
    # cl100k_base (GPT-4): 100_277,  # tiktoken byte-level BPE
    # LLaMA 3:             128_256,  # tiktoken-style BPE, extended vocab
}

for model_name, expected_size in vocab_sizes.items():
    tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
    actual = tok.vocab_size
    # Note: len(tok) includes added special tokens; vocab_size is the base size
    print(f"{model_name:30s}  vocab_size={actual:>7,}  len={len(tok):>7,}")

# tiktoken vocabularies
for enc_name in ["gpt2", "cl100k_base", "o200k_base"]:
    enc = tiktoken.get_encoding(enc_name)
    print(f"tiktoken {enc_name:20s}  n_vocab={enc.n_vocab:>7,}")
# tiktoken gpt2                   n_vocab=  50,257
# tiktoken cl100k_base            n_vocab= 100,277  (GPT-4, Claude-class)
# tiktoken o200k_base             n_vocab= 200,019  (GPT-4o and later)
Inspecting a Vocabulary

from transformers import AutoTokenizer

tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")

# The vocabulary is a dict: token_string → integer_id
vocab = tok.get_vocab()
print(f"Vocabulary size: {len(vocab):,}")

# Look up specific tokens
for token in ["[PAD]", "[UNK]", "[CLS]", "[SEP]", "[MASK]",
              "the", "##ing", "##tion", "running"]:
    token_id = vocab.get(token, "NOT FOUND")
    print(f"  {token!r:20s} → {token_id}")
# '[PAD]'              → 0
# '[UNK]'             → 100
# '[CLS]'             → 101
# '[SEP]'             → 102
# '[MASK]'            → 103
# 'the'               → 1996   (very common → low ID)
# '##ing'             → 2075
# '##tion'            → 3508
# 'running'           → 2770   (whole word in vocab)

# Reverse lookup: id → token
id_to_token = {v: k for k, v in vocab.items()}
print(id_to_token[1000])   # one of the early tokens by frequency

# Token frequency distribution: low IDs are generally more common
# (tokenisers often sort by frequency during vocabulary construction)
low_id_tokens  = [id_to_token[i] for i in range(1000, 1010)]
high_id_tokens = [id_to_token[i] for i in range(29_000, 29_010)]
print("Low  IDs:", low_id_tokens)   # common words
print("High IDs:", high_id_tokens)  # rare subwords or punctuation combos
Token Fertility: Measuring Vocabulary Efficiency

Fertility is the average number of tokens produced per word for a given language. A vocabulary trained primarily on English produces low fertility for English (≈0.75 tokens per word for common words) but high fertility for other languages — a single Chinese character may expand to 2–3 tokens because the vocabulary has few dedicated CJK entries. This directly affects cost and context efficiency for multilingual applications:

import tiktoken

enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")  # GPT-4 tokeniser

# Compare token counts for equivalent content across languages
sentences = {
    "English":  "The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog.",
    "French":   "Le renard brun rapide saute par-dessus le chien paresseux.",
    "Japanese": "速い茶色のキツネが怠け者の犬を飛び越えた。",
    "Arabic":   "القفز فوق الكلب الكسول من قبل الثعلب البني السريع.",
    "Hindi":    "तेज़ भूरी लोमड़ी आलसी कुत्ते के ऊपर कूद गई।",
}

for lang, text in sentences.items():
    tokens = enc.encode(text)
    words = len(text.split())
    fertility = len(tokens) / max(words, 1)
    print(f"{lang:10s}  {len(tokens):3d} tokens / {words:2d} words  fertility={fertility:.2f}")

# English      9 tokens / 9 words  fertility=1.00
# French      14 tokens / 9 words  fertility=1.56
# Japanese    20 tokens / 8 words  fertility=2.50
# Arabic      17 tokens / 8 words  fertility=2.13
# Hindi       22 tokens / 9 words  fertility=2.44
#
# Implication: the same factual content in Japanese costs ~2.5× more tokens
# (and therefore more inference cost) than in English with cl100k_base.
The Embedding Matrix in Code

import torch
import torch.nn as nn


class TokenEmbedding(nn.Module):
    """
    The lookup table that converts token IDs to dense vectors.
    This is the first layer of every transformer.
    """
    def __init__(self, vocab_size: int, d_model: int):
        super().__init__()
        self.embed = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, d_model)
        # Weight initialisation matters: small std keeps early activations stable
        nn.init.normal_(self.embed.weight, mean=0.0, std=d_model ** -0.5)

    def forward(self, token_ids: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        # token_ids: (batch, seq_len)  → output: (batch, seq_len, d_model)
        return self.embed(token_ids)


# Parameter count for the embedding matrix alone
configs = [
    ("BERT-base",   30_522,  768),
    ("GPT-2",       50_257,  768),
    ("LLaMA 3 8B", 128_256, 4096),
    ("GPT-4 est.", 100_277, 8192),
]
for name, vocab_size, d_model in configs:
    params = vocab_size * d_model
    size_mb = params * 2 / 1_000_000   # BF16: 2 bytes per param
    print(f"{name:15s}  {vocab_size:>7,} × {d_model:>5,} = {params:>12,} params ({size_mb:>6.1f} MB)")

# BERT-base         30,522 ×   768 =    23,440,896 params (  46.9 MB)
# GPT-2             50,257 ×   768 =    38,597,376 params (  77.2 MB)
# LLaMA 3 8B      128,256 × 4,096 =   525,336,576 params (1050.7 MB)
# GPT-4 est.      100,277 × 8,192 =   821,469,184 params (1642.9 MB)

Vocabulary size is a parameter budget decision disguised as a tokeniser decision. A 128K vocabulary adds ~500M parameters to a model just in the embedding matrix — parameters that might otherwise go into attention layers. The right size depends on the language mix of the training data and the target context length: multilingual models training on dozens of languages need larger vocabularies to achieve reasonable fertility across scripts, while a monolingual English model can be competitive at 32K–50K. The trend toward 100K+ vocabularies in recent models reflects both the shift to multilingual capability and the realisation that efficient tokenisation reduces the effective context window cost for long documents.
What a Tokeniser Vocabulary Is

A tokeniser's vocabulary is a finite, fixed lookup table that maps every known token string to a unique non-negative integer, called its ID. The vocabulary is assembled once — during the tokeniser training phase, before the language model itself is trained — and then frozen. Every text the model will ever see must be expressible as a sequence of IDs drawn from this table. Bytes or characters that do not map to any vocabulary entry are handled by the unknown token, or in the case of byte-level BPE, by falling back to individual byte tokens that cover every possible byte value (0–255), making the vocabulary truly lossless.

The vocabulary is not just a software artefact — it is physically embedded in the model's parameter count. Two weight matrices in every transformer share the vocabulary's first dimension: the input embedding matrix (maps token ID to a dense vector) and the output unembedding matrix (maps the final hidden state back to a probability distribution over all vocabulary items). These are typically weight-tied, so they are literally the same tensor used in two directions. Choosing a vocabulary size of 32K versus 128K changes the number of trainable parameters by tens to hundreds of millions before the first attention layer is even considered.

Vocabulary design is inseparable from the tokenisation algorithm. WordPiece (BERT) learns a vocabulary from corpus frequency statistics and always keeps full words when they occur above a threshold, splitting rarer words into subwords prefixed with "##". Byte-Pair Encoding (GPT-2, LLaMA, GPT-4) starts from individual characters or bytes and iteratively merges the most frequent adjacent pair until the target vocabulary size is reached. SentencePiece Unigram (T5, ALBERT) instead starts from a large candidate set and prunes tokens whose removal costs the least in terms of the data's likelihood. Each approach produces a vocabulary with different coverage properties, special-token placement conventions, and multilingual behaviour.
Why Vocabulary Size Matters

Vocabulary size is one of the highest-leverage architectural decisions in an LLM's design because its effects ripple through training cost, inference cost, context window utilisation, multilingual capability, and fine-tuning complexity simultaneously. A poor choice cannot be corrected without retraining the tokeniser and re-pretaining the model from scratch — there is no cheap migration path.

Consider a concrete business impact: if a model serves a global SaaS product where 40% of users write in languages other than English, and the model's vocabulary was trained predominantly on English text, those users pay 2–3× more per API call (because their text tokenises into 2–3× more tokens), and they also receive worse quality because the model has seen fewer training examples per token for their languages. The vocabulary is the root cause of both problems.

The trend from 30K (BERT, 2018) to 200K (GPT-4o, 2024) vocabularies reflects hard-won lessons: richer vocabularies produce shorter sequences for equivalent content, which stretches the effective range of a fixed context window, reduces softmax compute at inference, and improves multilingual parity — all at the cost of a larger embedding matrix. Understanding this trade-off is essential when selecting a base model for fine-tuning, estimating API costs for multilingual applications, or designing a custom tokeniser for a domain-specific model.
Benefits of a Well-Sized Vocabulary
Shorter sequences for the same content.

A 128K vocabulary can represent common English words and frequent code tokens as single tokens, while a 30K vocabulary must split them into 2–3 subword pieces. Shorter sequences mean more content fits in the model's context window and every attention computation is cheaper.
Multilingual parity.

A large vocabulary trained on balanced multilingual data allocates tokens to scripts proportional to their usage, reducing the token-cost penalty for non-Latin languages. Models with 100K+ vocabularies show fertility rates for CJK, Arabic, and Devanagari that are 30–50% better than 30K English-centric vocabularies.
Better representation of rare proper nouns and technical terms.

A larger vocabulary can assign single-token IDs to domain-specific terminology — medical Latin, legal jargon, programming language keywords — that a smaller vocabulary fragments into unintuitive subword sequences, potentially degrading the model's reasoning about those terms.
Code tokenisation efficiency.

Vocabularies like cl100k_base and o200k_base contain many common programming identifiers and punctuation patterns as single tokens, significantly reducing the token count for code relative to older vocabularies that split identifiers into syllables.
Lossless encoding via byte-level fallback.

Byte-level BPE vocabularies include all 256 raw byte tokens, ensuring that any Unicode text — including emoji, rare scripts, and binary content — can be encoded without dropping characters, unlike character-level vocabularies that rely on an UNK token.

attrs.alt: Vocabulary token ID pipeline and size comparison

content.text: How Vocabulary Construction Works

BPE vocabulary construction is a greedy, bottom-up merge process. It begins by treating every character (or byte, for byte-level BPE) in the training corpus as a separate token. It then repeatedly finds the pair of adjacent tokens that co-occurs most frequently across the entire corpus, adds that merged token to the vocabulary, and re-encodes all occurrences of the pair as the single merged token. This continues until the desired vocabulary size is reached. The merges are saved in a deterministic ordered list — the merge rules — and this list, combined with the initial character/byte set, is everything needed to reproduce the tokenisation of any text without rerunning the corpus statistics.
The output vocabulary size
equals the initial alphabet size plus the number of merge steps. For byte-level BPE, the initial alphabet is exactly 256 (one per byte), so
where

is the number of merges. GPT-2's 50,257 results from 256 bytes + 50,000 merges + 1 special token. LLaMA 3's 128,256 results from 256 + approximately 127,744 merges + 256 special-token slots reserved for future use.
The output sequence length after tokenisation depends on how well the vocabulary's merge rules compress the input. Formally, for a text of
characters, the expected token count
satisfies
where
is the average token length in characters. For English prose,
with cl100k_base. For Japanese,
(shorter average token length → more tokens → higher cost).
When Vocabulary Choice Matters (and When It Does Not)

Use a larger vocabulary (100K+) when: your application is multilingual or multi-script; you are working with code-heavy content where identifier and keyword sequences matter; or you have long-document workloads where every token saved translates directly to more content fitting in the context window. Models like GPT-4 (cl100k_base, 100K) and LLaMA 3 (128K) are good base choices for these scenarios.

Use a smaller vocabulary (30K–50K) when: your model is monolingual English; you have strict memory constraints; or you are training a small model where the embedding matrix is a significant fraction of the total parameter budget. For a 10M-parameter model, a 128K vocabulary with d_model=256 would require 128K × 256 = 32M parameters just for the embedding — three times the rest of the model. A 32K vocabulary is only 8M parameters, leaving 2M for attention and FFN layers.

Vocabulary choice does NOT matter significantly when: you are using an existing pretrained model via an API and have no control over the tokeniser; when your input data is already in a fixed token format; or when you are building a downstream application that consumes model outputs rather than training or fine-tuning a model.
Common Mistakes
Confusing len(tokenizer) with tokenizer.vocab_size.

The attribute vocab_size returns the base vocabulary size (before added special tokens). len(tokenizer) returns the total number of tokens including any special tokens added after loading. For fine-tuning, you must use len(tokenizer) when calling model.resize_token_embeddings() — using vocab_size will leave the newly added token IDs out of range.
Forgetting to resize the embedding matrix after adding special tokens.

Calling tokenizer.add_special_tokens() expands the tokeniser's vocabulary, but the model's embedding matrix still has the old number of rows. Encoding text with the new tokens and feeding it to the unchanged model will raise an index-out-of-range error. Always call model.resize_token_embeddings(len(tokenizer)) immediately after modifying the tokeniser.
Assuming token IDs are consistent across models.

The token string "the" has ID 1996 in BERT, ID 1169 in GPT-2, and a completely different ID in LLaMA 3. IDs are vocabulary-specific — never hardcode token IDs across models, and never copy input_ids tensors from one model's tokeniser to another.
Ignoring vocabulary size when estimating inference cost.

API providers charge per token, not per character. A Japanese query that is 50 characters long may cost 3× more tokens than the same factual content expressed in English when using a vocabulary with poor CJK coverage. Always benchmark token counts for your actual language distribution before committing to a model for a multilingual product.
Training a new tokeniser on a biased corpus.

If you train BPE on a corpus that is 90% English, the resulting vocabulary will be English-optimised even if you set vocab_size=100K. The merge rules will encode common English n-grams as single tokens while fragmenting common phrases in other languages into many small pieces. Vocabulary quality tracks corpus language distribution, not just size.

from transformers import AutoTokenizer

tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("gpt2")

# WRONG: uses vocab_size (base), misses added special tokens
# model.resize_token_embeddings(tok.vocab_size)  # BUG if tokens were added

# RIGHT: uses len(tok) to include any added special tokens
# model.resize_token_embeddings(len(tok))

# Demonstrate the difference:
print(f"vocab_size : {tok.vocab_size}")  # 50257
print(f"len(tok)   : {len(tok)}")        # 50257 (same, no tokens added yet)

tok.add_special_tokens({"additional_special_tokens": ["<|CUSTOM|>"]})
print(f"vocab_size : {tok.vocab_size}")  # 50257  (unchanged)
print(f"len(tok)   : {len(tok)}")        # 50258  (includes the new token)
# Always call model.resize_token_embeddings(len(tok)) after this!
Best Practices
Always read the model card for vocabulary details.
The vocabulary size, tokeniser algorithm, and special token conventions are model-specific. Never assume they match another model you have used previously.
Benchmark token counts for your actual data.

Before committing to a model for production, run your representative dataset through the tokeniser and measure average tokens per document. This directly informs your context budget and API cost estimates.
When fine-tuning, add special tokens before training — not after.

The newly initialised embedding rows for added tokens need gradient updates to become meaningful. If you add them after training, the model has never seen those IDs and will produce garbage for the corresponding embeddings.
Use tiktoken for fast production tokenisation.

The Hugging Face tokeniser runs in Python and is convenient for experimentation, but tiktoken (written in Rust, with Python bindings) is 5–10× faster for high-throughput tokenisation in production. It supports cl100k_base (GPT-4) and o200k_base (GPT-4o) natively.
For custom domain models, train the tokeniser on your actual domain corpus.

A general-purpose tokeniser tokenises biomedical text inefficiently because drug names and Latin anatomical terms are rare in general web corpora. A domain-specific tokeniser trained on PubMed, for example, encodes common medical terms as single tokens, improving both efficiency and model quality.
Performance and Scalability

Tokenisation throughput depends on three factors: the tokeniser implementation (Python vs Rust), the vocabulary algorithm (BPE requires following merge rules in order; WordPiece runs a max-match over the vocabulary trie), and the length of the input. For a 100K-token document, tiktoken tokenises at roughly 1–5 million tokens per second on a modern CPU, while the pure-Python Hugging Face fast tokeniser reaches 200K–500K tokens per second. The Rust-backed "fast" variant of AutoTokenizer (using tokenizers library) is competitive with tiktoken.

Softmax cost at the output head scales linearly with vocabulary size: computing logits over 200K tokens requires roughly 4× the floating-point operations per decoder step compared to 50K tokens (assuming the same d_model). For a model generating 500 tokens, this translates to 500 × 4× overhead on the final linear projection. Mitigations include: sampling with top-k or top-p (you can compute only the top-k logits with sorted partial softmax); speculative decoding (the draft model uses a smaller vocabulary); and quantised vocabularies that fuse the unembedding projection with the decode step.

import tiktoken, time

enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")

# Benchmark tokenisation speed
test_text = "The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. " * 10_000  # ~90K chars

reps = 5
times = []
for _ in range(reps):
    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    ids = enc.encode(test_text)
    times.append(time.perf_counter() - t0)

avg_ms = sum(times) / len(times) * 1000
tok_per_sec = len(ids) / (sum(times) / len(times))

print(f"Input chars    : {len(test_text):,}")
print(f"Output tokens  : {len(ids):,}")
print(f"Avg time       : {avg_ms:.1f} ms")
print(f"Throughput     : {tok_per_sec:,.0f} tokens/sec")
# Expected on a modern CPU: ~1–3 million tokens/sec for tiktoken

# Memory: the vocabulary serialised to disk
import sys
vocab = {enc.decode([i]): i for i in range(enc.n_vocab) if enc._special_tokens.get(enc.decode([i])) is None}
print(f"Vocabulary entries : {len(vocab):,}")
# The in-memory vocabulary trie is the lookup structure; serialised size ≈ a few MB
Key Takeaways
The vocabulary is a frozen lookup table mapping token strings to integer IDs, built once during tokeniser training and shared between input embedding and output unembedding matrices.
Vocabulary size directly controls the embedding matrix dimensions and therefore the model's parameter count — larger vocabulary means millions more parameters at the input and output layers.

Larger vocabularies produce shorter sequences for the same text (higher compression), improving context window utilisation and reducing inference cost per document, but at the cost of a larger embedding matrix.

Token fertility — tokens per word — is the key metric for measuring vocabulary efficiency across languages; a vocabulary trained primarily on English will have high fertility (3–5× more tokens) for CJK, Arabic, and other scripts.
BPE merges the most frequent adjacent token pair iteratively; the merge rules list is everything needed to reproduce tokenisation deterministically on any future text.
Always use len(tokenizer) rather than tokenizer.vocab_size when resizing the model embedding matrix after adding special tokens — the two values diverge as soon as any token is added.
For production tokenisation, use tiktoken (Rust-backed) over the Python Hugging Face tokeniser for 5–10× throughput improvement at scale.

The trend from 30K (BERT, 2018) to 200K (GPT-4o, 2024) reflects the shift to multilingual capability and the recognition that efficient tokenisation stretches the effective context window for real-world diverse inputs.
